title: Real Box Breathing
content: Box Breathing in the Real World
Box breathing is a simple technique used to control breathing and regulate the nervous system.
It is used by some of the top special forces units in the world to stay calm and focused under pressure.
The method is straightforward:
Breathe in through your nose for four seconds.
Hold for four seconds.
Breathe out through your mouth for four seconds.
Hold again for four seconds.
Repeat the cycle.
Now bring that into something ordinary.
You step into an elevator.
A few people are already inside.
More get in.
It is quiet. Close.
You can feel yourself becoming aware of your breathing.
A little tight in the chest. Slight edge of discomfort.
Nothing is wrong, but your body is reacting.
This is where most people either ignore it or try to push it away.
Instead, you do something different.
You start box breathing.
In for four.
Hold for four.
Out for four.
Hold for four.
No one knows you are doing it.
Your shoulders drop slightly.
Your breathing steadies.
The feeling does not disappear instantly, but it softens.
You are no longer caught in it.
You are observing it.
The elevator stops.
People step out.
You step out.
Same situation. Different experience.
That is the point.
Box breathing is not reserved for extreme situations.
It is there for the small moments where your body starts to drift out of balance.
Used early, it keeps things simple.
Used often, it becomes automatic.
It is a quiet tool.
But it gives you control when it matters.

title: A poem by John O'Donohue
content: FOR THE FAMILY AND FRIENDS OF A SUICIDE
by John O'Donohue
As you huddle around the torn silence,
Each by this lonely deed exiled
To a solitary confinement of soul,
May some small glow from what has been lost
Return like the kindness of candlelight.
As your eyes strain to sift
This sudden wall of dark
And no one can say why
In such a forsaken, secret way,
This death was sent for …
May one of the lovely hours
Of memory return
Like a field of ease
Among these gravelled days.
May the Angel of Wisdom
Enter this ruin of absence
And guide your minds
To receive this bitter chalice
So that you do not damage yourselves
By attending only at the hungry altar
Of regret and anger and guilt.
May you be given some inkling
That there could be something else at work
And that what to you now seems
Dark, destructive and forlorn,
Might be a destiny that looks different
From inside the eternal script.
May vision be granted to you
To see this with the eyes of providence.
May your loss become a sanctuary
Where new presence will dwell
To refine and enrich
The rest of your life
With courage and compassion.
And may your lost loved one
Enter into the beauty of eternal tranquillity,
In that place where there is no more sorrow
Or separation or mourning or tears.
From the collection "To Bless The Space Between Us"
(a.k.a. "Benedictus") by John O'Donohue

title: Making Time
content: Making Time
It can be difficult to live a life when all of your time feels spoken for.
There are always things pulling at you.
Your job.
Obligations.
Requests from other people.
You might be a parent.
You might be part of a club or a community.
You might be helping to care for someone who is unwell.
You might have a friend who is struggling. They may not even ask for help, but you give your time anyway because you care deeply about them.
And that care comes from a good place. From love.
But when everything is pulling at you, it can begin to feel like there is very little left for yourself.
It is not the hours that are difficult to find.
It is the minutes.

Sometimes it can feel hard just to carve out a little space for yourself in the day. But it is important that we do. We need time to look after ourselves, to nurture ourselves, and to consider our own wellbeing and our own mental health.
Someone once said that if you do not make time for your wellbeing, you will be forced to make time for your sickness.
There is truth in that. When we become sick, everything stops. The body makes the decision for us. We have no choice but to rest and recover.
But perhaps it does not have to reach that point.
Perhaps we can meet it halfway.
We can care for ourselves before we become physically unwell.
And before we become emotionally exhausted.
Recently I reached out to a counsellor and spoke with them for a couple of months during a difficult time in my life. It helped me more than I expected.
It was something I never thought I would do. But I did.
And I am glad that I did.
If you have never spoken to someone in that way before, it may feel like a big step. But it can also be a brave one, and sometimes a very helpful one.
So if you can, carve out a little time for yourself.
Protect a few minutes of the day that belong only to you.
You are worth that time.
It matters more than you might think.

title: It's just a missing book....... that's all.
content: book in a Teacup
It’s incredible how quickly things can change inside the mind and body.
Last night I was feeling completely fine. Nothing unusual. Just an ordinary evening. Then I went to look for a book that a friend had lent me. I had promised I would look after it.
I couldn’t find it.

At first it seemed like nothing, but the longer I searched the more unsettled I became. I started going through the house room by room. I checked shelves, tables, bags, anywhere it might have been. Eventually I even went outside and emptied the bin just in case it had somehow been thrown away by mistake.

By that point I could feel my body reacting. My heart was racing. My thoughts were jumping all over the place. It felt like I had gone straight into fight or flight mode over something as simple as a missing book.
It’s strange how the nervous system works. In that moment it felt very serious.
Standing in the kitchen, I stopped for a moment and tried a few minutes of box breathing.
Inhale.
Hold.
Exhale.
Hold.
After a couple of minutes I could feel my body settling again. My breathing slowed. My thoughts became clearer.
And then a very simple thought came to me.
I had probably left the book at work.
When I arrived this morning, there it was.
What felt like a big problem the night before turned out to be a storm in a teacup.
Sometimes all it takes is a small pause to let the mind and body find their way back to calm.

title: Old Friends, Strong Ties
content: Old Friends, Strong Ties
Why Lifelong Friendship Matters for Mental Wellbeing
Friendship is one of the strongest protections we have for our mental health. Old friends and shared history remind us who we are, especially during times of change, loneliness, or uncertainty.
Whenever I left Canada and moved to Ireland, it was a difficult time in my life.
In one way, I severed ties with many of my old friends, even without meaning to. Life just carried on. Distance grew. Time passed.

Moving to a new place forced me to start again. I had to make new contacts, build new friendships, and learn a new place from scratch. It was uncomfortable. At times I felt anxious. There is a particular kind of loneliness that can come with starting over in a new country.
But something good happened too.
I met wonderful people here in Ireland. People who helped me feel at home. People who treated me like family and helped me build new connections in my life.
Still, I left my best friend back home. My high school friend. My childhood friend.
And there are no connections like old connections.
No friends like old friends.
So long as those connections are healthy ones, they are worth looking after. They are worth tending to.
There is a feeling you get when you have not spoken to an old friend for a long time, but when you do, it feels like you spoke yesterday. That is how you know the connection runs deep.
Sometimes my friend will send a small message.
Or a picture.
Or the second part of a joke that only the two of us understand.
In those small moments I realise again how important that connection is.

When I am feeling good and life is going well, I send him a message and tell him how things are. And if I slide into a darker place, if I am feeling the weight of life, stress, or anxiety, those same connections are the ones I lean on.
I understand that now.
I did not understand it when I was younger.
Strong friendships are part of our resilience. They are part of our wellbeing. They help keep us connected to the world when life becomes difficult.
Looking after relationships is not always easy. It takes time. It takes effort. Sometimes it just takes sending a message.
But those connections matter.
Sometimes the message you send to an old friend is more important than you realise.
Sometimes it reminds both of you that you are not alone.

title: a chance encounter with a remarkable person
content: Recently I had the opportunity to spend time with an extraordinary man, Philip O’Keefe from Northern Ireland. Philip is now 85 years old and the author of a book on suicide titled Analyzing Suicide.

Philip’s interest in the subject is deeply personal. He lost two of his brothers to suicide, and those losses stayed with him throughout his life. After a full career as a history teacher, Philip returned to the University of Ulster in retirement. There he undertook years of study which eventually led to the completion of his PhD. Out of more than a decade of research, reflection, and lived experience, Analyzing Suicide emerged.

The book is deeply personal. Anyone who reads it, particularly those from Northern Ireland, will likely recognise how close the subject feels. Many of the case studies involve people from Northern Ireland, some as recent as 2022. It is difficult to read the book without feeling affected by it.

What struck me most when speaking with Philip was the way he talks about suicide. His perspective spans generations. His language is thoughtful and reflective, sometimes using words and ideas that are not often heard in modern conversations about suicide. After more than sixty-five years of thinking about the subject through lived experience, teaching, research, and writing, his reflections carry a certain depth that is hard to ignore.
I deeply enjoyed my conversation with Philip, and I know it will stay with me for a long time. I now count him as a friend.

One thing that stayed with me afterwards was the idea of isolation. It is something many of us experience in different ways, and something we all need to work to overcome. Meeting people, forming connections, and taking the time to listen to one another can change how we see the world.
My conversation with Philip reminded me of that. It left me feeling reflective, and grateful that I had the chance to meet someone like him.

title: Why Pause Matters
content: Sometimes the mind moves too quickly.
Thoughts stack on top of each other. Regret. Fear. Shame. Exhaustion.
Before long it can feel like everything is closing in.
When that happens the mind starts looking for a way out. Not because someone wants to die, but because they want the pain to stop.
That is where a pause can help.
A pause is not a solution.
It is not therapy.
It is not someone telling you what to do.
It is simply a moment where the speed slows down.

title: You Don’t Need to Solve Your Whole Life Tonight
content: When things feel overwhelming, the mind often tries to solve everything at once.
All the problems. All the regrets. All the fears about the future.
The truth is that no human being can solve their entire life in one night.
Sometimes the most powerful step is much smaller.
Drink some water.
Step outside.
Send a message to someone you trust.
Small steps create movement.
And movement creates possibility.
